contributingFactors: NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` uses `podSelector: {}` which selects ALL pods in the namespace, not just specific targets
NetworkPolicy was injected 29 seconds AFTER deployment creation (18:03:02Z vs 18:03:31Z), explaining initial `probe=success` entries before policy took effect
The NetworkPolicy has NO ingress rules — it is a pure deny-all with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and empty spec
`traffic-gen` readiness probe times out (3s max) because ingress to `web-frontend` pods on port 8080 is completely blocked
25 `Unhealthy` pod events with `Readiness probe failed: command timed out` confirmed from 18:03:17Z to 18:05:24Z
`web-frontend` pods are themselves healthy (localhost probes pass), confirming network-level ingress blocking is the sole cause
NetworkPolicy labeled `injected-by: kubernaut-demo` indicating deliberate injection as part of a demo scenario
rootCauseAnalysis.summary: A `deny-all-ingress` NetworkPolicy (injected 29 seconds after pod start) blocks all ingress traffic to all pods in `demo-netpol` via an empty `podSelector: {}` with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and no ingress rules. This causes the `traffic-gen` readiness probe (`curl http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`) to time out, making the pod unready and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch (0/1 ready replicas).

analysis.rootCauseSummary: A `deny-all-ingress` NetworkPolicy (injected 29 seconds after pod start) blocks all ingress traffic to all pods in `demo-netpol` via an empty `podSelector: {}` with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and no ingress rules. This causes the `traffic-gen` readiness probe (`curl http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`) to time out, making the pod unready and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch (0/1 ready replicas).

selectedWorkflow.rationale: fix-network-policy-v1 is purpose-built to remove deny-all NetworkPolicies blocking legitimate ingress traffic and causing health check failures — exactly matching this incident. The workflow's `detectedLabels` includes `networkIsolated: true`, which aligns with the enrichment context. Severity (critical), environment (staging), and component (networkpolicy/deployment) labels all match. The `POLICY_NAME` parameter is supplied explicitly to target `deny-all-ingress` directly, bypassing any auto-detection ambiguity.

# Pod Summary — Namespace: `demo-netpol`

## Overview
- **Total Pods:** 3
- **Namespace:** `demo-netpol`
- **Cluster CNI:** OVN-Kubernetes (via Multus), OpenShift SCC: `restricted-v2`

---

## Pod 1 — `traffic-gen-6b4c45cb57-ttstc` ⚠️ NOT READY

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Owner** | ReplicaSet `traffic-gen-6b4c45cb57` |
| **Node** | `dev-worker-0.redhat-internal.com` |
| **Phase** | Running |
| **Ready** | ❌ False (`ContainersNotReady`) |
| **Created** | 2026-05-03T18:03:02Z |
| **Container** | `curl` (image: `curlimages/curl:8.12.1`) |
| **Container Ready** | ❌ False |
| **Container Started** | ✅ Yes (18:03:09Z) |
| **Restart Count** | 0 |
| **Resources** | req: 5m CPU / 16Mi mem; lim: 20m CPU / 32Mi mem |
| **QoS** | Burstable |

### ⚠️ Key Incident Detail
- The `curl` container is **running but failing its readiness probe**
- Readiness probe: `curl -sf -o /dev/null -m 3 http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`
- The container loop also probes the same endpoint every 5s and logs `probe=success` or `probe=failure`
- Pod became unready at **18:03:42Z** — ~40 seconds after start
- **Strongly suggests the `curl` pod cannot reach `web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`** — likely a NetworkPolicy blocking ingress/egress

---

---

## Incident Investigation Notes

| Observation | Significance |
|---|---|
| Both `web-frontend` pods are healthy and their own probes pass | The web server itself is functioning |
| `traffic-gen` readiness probe targets the same endpoint and **fails** | Traffic from `traffic-gen` to `web-frontend` is being blocked |
| `traffic-gen` and one `web-frontend` pod share the same node (`dev-worker-0`) | Issue is not node-level isolation |
| `traffic-gen` has `restartCount: 0` and container is running | Not a crash/OOM issue |
| Namespace is `demo-netpol` | Name strongly implies NetworkPolicy testing — a blocking policy is the primary suspect |
| All pods use `restricted-v2` SCC, drop ALL capabilities | Security context is consistent and unlikely to be the cause |

### Recommended Next Steps
1. **Inspect NetworkPolicies** in `demo-netpol` — check for missing ingress rules on `web-frontend` allowing traffic from `app=traffic-gen`
2. **Check for egress restrictions** on `traffic-gen` blocking port 8080
3. Verify the `web-frontend` Service exists and correctly selects `app=web-frontend` pods on port 8080
4. Test connectivity directly: `kubectl exec traffic-gen-6b4c45cb57-ttstc -n demo-netpol -- curl -v http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`

kaResponse.analysis: A `deny-all-ingress` NetworkPolicy (injected 29 seconds after pod start) blocks all ingress traffic to all pods in `demo-netpol` via an empty `podSelector: {}` with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and no ingress rules. This causes the `traffic-gen` readiness probe (`curl http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`) to time out, making the pod unready and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch (0/1 ready replicas).

rootCauseAnalysis.summary: A `deny-all-ingress` NetworkPolicy (injected 29 seconds after pod start) blocks all ingress traffic to all pods in `demo-netpol` via an empty `podSelector: {}` with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and no ingress rules. This causes the `traffic-gen` readiness probe (`curl http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz`) to time out, making the pod unready and triggering KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch (0/1 ready replicas).
causalChain: Signal: KubeDeploymentReplicasMismatch — Deployment `traffic-gen` in `demo-netpol` has 0/1 ready replicas
Why? Pod `traffic-gen-6b4c45cb57-ttstc` is Running but not Ready — readiness probe is failing with `command timed out`
Why? The readiness probe `curl http://web-frontend.demo-netpol:8080/healthz` times out — it cannot reach the `web-frontend` service
Why? NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` (podSelector: {}, policyTypes: [Ingress], no ingress rules) blocks ALL ingress traffic to ALL pods in the `demo-netpol` namespace

Root cause: NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` was injected at 18:03:31Z with a blanket deny-all ingress rule and no allowlist entries, completely blocking `traffic-gen` from reaching `web-frontend:8080`

dueDiligence.targetAccuracy: The remediation target is `NetworkPolicy/deny-all-ingress` because it is the resource whose configuration (or deletion) directly fixes the connectivity block. The Deployment `traffic-gen` is the symptom reporter, not the cause. The NetworkPolicy is the resource that needs to be modified (add ingress allow rule) or deleted.
dueDiligence.proportionality: The NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` is the single, specific, targeted resource causing the issue. Deleting it or adding an ingress allow rule is a well-defined, minimal fix. The Deployment does not need modification.
dueDiligence.scopeCompleteness: Investigated: Deployment spec and status, ReplicaSet (via pod owner reference), Pod describe + logs + events, all NetworkPolicies in namespace. Did not retrieve the `web-frontend` Service object (permission error for Knative API group), but this gap does not affect the conclusion since the web-frontend pods themselves are healthy.
dueDiligence.causalCompleteness: Traced fully: signal → pod not ready → readiness probe timeout → network connectivity blocked → deny-all NetworkPolicy. The NetworkPolicy spec is explicit (empty ingress rules, empty podSelector), leaving no ambiguity. Cannot go deeper — the policy is the terminal cause.
dueDiligence.evidenceSufficiency: All claims are backed by tool evidence: (1) Pod logs show probe=success then probe=failure transition, (2) Pod events confirm 25 `Readiness probe failed: command timed out` events, (3) NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` confirmed with empty podSelector and no ingress rules, (4) Deployment status shows `unavailableReplicas: 1` and condition `MinimumReplicasUnavailable`. Service listing was blocked by a permissions error (Knative API group) — however, `web-frontend` pods being healthy with passing probes confirms the service/app layer is not the issue.
dueDiligence.regressionAwareness: No prior remediation history available for `deny-all-ingress` NetworkPolicy. The resource context shows empty remediation history.
dueDiligence.alternativeHypotheses: Considered: (1) Application failure in web-frontend — ruled out: both web-frontend pods are Ready with passing liveness/readiness probes. (2) Node-level issue — ruled out: traffic-gen and one web-frontend pod share the same node (dev-worker-0) and the web-frontend pod is healthy. (3) Resource exhaustion (CPU/memory) — ruled out: restartCount=0, no OOM events, resources well within limits. (4) DNS resolution failure — ruled out: probe times out (not DNS NXDOMAIN), and initial probe=success entries confirm DNS was working before the NetworkPolicy was applied.
dueDiligence.confidenceCalibration: Starting at 1.0. -0.03 for inability to verify the `web-frontend` Service object (permission error). -0.02 for not being able to confirm whether any egress NetworkPolicy also exists (only one NetworkPolicy found, which only covers Ingress). Final confidence: 0.95.

contributingFactors: NetworkPolicy `deny-all-ingress` uses `podSelector: {}` which selects ALL pods in the namespace, not just specific targets
NetworkPolicy was injected 29 seconds AFTER deployment creation (18:03:02Z vs 18:03:31Z), explaining initial `probe=success` entries before policy took effect
The NetworkPolicy has NO ingress rules — it is a pure deny-all with `policyTypes: [Ingress]` and empty spec
`traffic-gen` readiness probe times out (3s max) because ingress to `web-frontend` pods on port 8080 is completely blocked
25 `Unhealthy` pod events with `Readiness probe failed: command timed out` confirmed from 18:03:17Z to 18:05:24Z
`web-frontend` pods are themselves healthy (localhost probes pass), confirming network-level ingress blocking is the sole cause
NetworkPolicy labeled `injected-by: kubernaut-demo` indicating deliberate injection as part of a demo scenario
